projects.name: Solar + Energy Storage on Garage
projects.description: Install 5-7 solar panels on south-facing garage roof face (20x12 ft, ~2-2.8 kW) plus battery storage. Solar = standalone ROI investment (~5yr payback at $0.42/kWh). Battery = generator alternative (no lot space for generator). 30% federal ITC applies to both when paired.
projects.notes: DESIGN: South garage face only. 5 portrait panels = 2.0 kW; 7 mixed = 2.8 kW. ~3,200-4,500 kWh/yr production (20-26% offset). All-in after ITC: solar ~$9-10k, battery ~$9-10k. VENDORS: Sunergy (sunergy.com) — EnergySage 2026 CA Installer of Year, Enphase Platinum, no door-to-door. NRG Clean Power (nrgcleanpower.com) — Diamond Certified, 35yr, San Mateo County, 40yr warranty. Tesla Energy (tesla.com/energy) — online only. AVOID: Sunrun.

tasks.title: Analyze energy usage and size system
tasks.notes: 17,178 kWh/yr at $0.42/kWh avg. Target: 5-7 panels south garage face (20x12 ft) = 2-2.8 kW. ~5yr payback after ITC.

tasks.title: Research and shortlist solar installers
tasks.notes: Shortlist: Sunergy (sunergy.com, Enphase Platinum, no door-to-door), NRG Clean Power (nrgcleanpower.com, Diamond Certified, San Mateo County), Tesla Energy (tesla.com/energy, online only). Avoid Sunrun.

tasks.title: Contact installers for quotes
tasks.notes: Spec: south garage face (20x12 ft, 5-7 panels, ~2-2.8 kW), paired battery (Enphase IQ or Powerwall 3), detached garage sub-panel interconnect. Ask each for: $/W, panel brand, inverter type, battery spec, warranty, timeline, ITC total. Request free shade/LIDAR analysis.

tasks.title: Compare quotes and select installer
tasks.notes: Prefer Enphase microinverters over string inverter. Compare $/W, battery spec, warranty, timeline. 30% ITC on full system incl. battery.

tasks.title: Confirm ITC timing — credit files in year PTO is granted
tasks.notes: 30% federal ITC on Form 5695. Claimed in tax year Permission to Operate granted by PG&E. Battery qualifies only when solar-paired. Plan install timing accordingly.

needs_attention.title: Contact installers for quotes

insights.summary: The sustained 12–13 device count with zero camera motion indicates the household is settled indoors with numerous persistently connected IoT devices, and the recent spike to 17–18 devices likely reflects normal evening device activation or a guest network connecting. This pattern is consistent with a typical Friday night at home and poses no security concern.

The sustained 12–14 device count paired with zero camera motion for four hours strongly suggests either unexpected guests are occupying a camera-blind area or the presence system is miscounting IoT devices, warranting a quick verification to rule out unmonitored occupancy or a sensor calibration issue.

The persistent 12–14 device count with zero camera motion over four hours indicates the presence system is likely tracking idle IoT gadgets or devices left charging rather than active occupants, which matters for accurate occupancy tracking and suggests the presence algorithm may need calibration.

The persistent 13–14 device count with zero camera motion on a Friday evening strongly suggests the security cameras are offline or malfunctioning, as a family of this size would typically generate movement. Verify camera status to ensure home monitoring is active.

The sustained 13–19 device count with zero camera motion over four hours definitively confirms a network presence detection anomaly (likely Wi-Fi scanning artifacts or IoT noise) rather than actual occupancy, indicating the presence system requires calibration to prevent false occupancy reports.

The sustained high device count (peaking at 21) with zero camera motion indicates no physical occupancy is driving the network load, strongly pointing to an IoT surge, guest network activation, or a rogue device. Steve should verify his router’s connected device list to rule out unauthorized access or a misconfigured smart hub.

The sustained 17–19 device count with zero camera motion over four hours strongly suggests a network misconfiguration or phantom device tracking rather than actual occupancy, as real human presence would typically trigger at least occasional camera activity or device movement. This matters because unreliable presence data can cause automations to malfunction and mask the true state of the home.

The sustained 13–17 device count far exceeds the household's six members, and combined with zero camera motion on a Friday afternoon, strongly suggests an unannounced gathering or guests are occupying camera-blind areas. This confirms the earlier correlations and indicates you may want to verify guest arrangements or check for sensor coverage gaps.

The persistent 15–17 device count paired with zero camera motion over four hours on a Friday afternoon strongly suggests the presence tracker is misclassifying smart home IoT devices as mobile phones, rather than indicating actual occupancy; this could lead to incorrect automations or missed security alerts.

The sustained high device count (15-19) combined with zero camera motion on a Friday afternoon indicates the household is likely present but the camera system may be offline, in sleep mode, or experiencing a network reporting failure. Verifying camera status is recommended to ensure security and pet monitoring are functioning.
